text.title: Junior AI-Overreliance Coaching Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every senior dev coaching a junior on AI overreliance must follow. These rules prevent two failures: missing the problem and over-coaching it.

rule.statement: Before raising overreliance in conversation, identify ≥ 1 specific pattern from the library: mismatched_naming, library_of_the_day, undefended_exception_flow, copy_pasted_prompt_artifact, no_comment_integration, unjustified_dependency. Vague "this looks AI-generated" feedback is rejected.
rule.rationale: Specific behavioral feedback ("you imported `axios` despite the project standardizing on `fetch`") changes behavior; vague vibes-feedback raises defensiveness without learning.

rule.statement: Open every coaching exchange with a question, not a verdict: "Walk me through why X library here", "What did you consider before catching this exception?". Reject coaching openings that state the junior's mistake before they explain.
rule.rationale: Questions that invite reasoning give the junior space to discover the gap themselves; verdicts trigger defensiveness and slow learning.

rule.statement: When pattern is detected, ask the junior: "If your AI assistant were down for 24 hours, how would you have solved this?" Use the response as the diagnostic for coaching depth. Reject coaching that skips this calibration step.
rule.rationale: The answer reveals whether the junior has a fallback or fully depends on the tool. The calibration shapes whether coaching is "small nudge" or "deep pairing."

rule.statement: PR comments use delta format: "Here's what the change does → here's the failure I'd worry about → here's the question I'd ask before approving." Reject snark, hot-takes, or one-line dismissals.
rule.rationale: Delta format teaches the reviewer's thinking, not just the verdict. Juniors absorb the pattern of senior reasoning over time.

rule.statement: Use the ladder: (1) acknowledge in passing → (2) ask in PR comment → (3) block PR + 1:1 pairing → (4) escalate to manager. Move one step per repeated occurrence. Reject jumping straight to escalation OR staying at acknowledge after 3+ occurrences.
rule.rationale: Graduated steps preserve relationship while creating real consequences. Skipping steps damages trust; never escalating fails the team.

example.title: Pattern-led delta review comment
example.code:
diff line: catch (e) { console.log(e); return null; }
Comment: "What the change does: catches any error from the API and returns null. Failure I'd worry about: callers will see null but won't know if it's 'request failed' vs 'no data exists.' This will hide bugs later. Question before approving: in your handler at line 47, how should the caller distinguish? What if you had to write this without AI — what's your habit for distinguishing user-data-empty vs system-error?"
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r3, r4: specific pattern (undefended_exception_flow), question opening, what-without-AI calibration, delta format.

example.title: Vague snarky review
example.code: "Looks AI-generated. Please think before you commit."
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r4 violations. No specific pattern named, no question, no delta format, no learning surface for the junior.
